questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: for a real-valued function of one real variable, what does its derivative represent at a point?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Geometrically it is the slope of the tangent line.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Geometrically it is the slope of the tangent line.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Geometrically it is the slope of the tangent line.

answers.text: The way I understand it: the instantaneous rate of change when the derivative exists.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The instantaneous rate of change when the derivative exists.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The instantaneous rate of change when the derivative exists.

answers.text: Short version: Definite integration, not differentiation, describes accumulated signed area.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Definite integration, not differentiation, describes accumulated signed area.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Definite integration, not differentiation, describes accumulated signed area.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: for a positive integer n, what is the derivative of `x^n`?

answers.text: The way I understand it: `n x^(n-1)`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. `n x^(n-1)`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. `n x^(n-1)`.

answers.text: Short version: `x^(n+1)/(n+1)`.

feedback.legit: Not this one. That expression is an antiderivative, not the derivative.
feedback.sus: Good catch. That expression is an antiderivative, not the derivative.

answers.text: I'd go with this: The exponent decreases by one and multiplies the result.

feedback.sus: This one is legit. The exponent decreases by one and multiplies the result.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: does a definite integral always equal ordinary geometric area?

answers.text: The way I understand it: it represents accumulated signed change.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It represents accumulated signed change.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It represents accumulated signed change.

answers.text: Short version: If a definite integral is zero, the function must be zero everywhere on the interval.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Positive and negative contributions can cancel, so a zero integral does not force the function to vanish.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Positive and negative contributions can cancel, so a zero integral does not force the function to vanish.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Yes, it is always nonnegative.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A definite integral is signed; regions below the axis contribute negatively.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A definite integral is signed; regions below the axis contribute negatively.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: when is the chain rule used?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, the outer derivative is multiplied by the derivative of the inner function.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The outer derivative is multiplied by the derivative of the inner function.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The outer derivative is multiplied by the derivative of the inner function.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: When differentiating a composition of functions.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. When differentiating a composition of functions.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. When differentiating a composition of functions.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Products use the product rule; compositions use the chain rule.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Products use the product rule; compositions use the chain rule.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Products use the product rule; compositions use the chain rule.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what is the derivative of a product `f(x)g(x)`?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: `f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. `f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. `f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)`.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: `f'(x)g'(x)`.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Differentiating each factor and multiplying omits two required terms.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Differentiating each factor and multiplying omits two required terms.

answers.text: From what I've seen, if one factor is constant, the derivative of the whole product must be zero.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A constant factor remains and multiplies the derivative of the other factor.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A constant factor remains and multiplies the derivative of the other factor.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: what connection does the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus make?

answers.text: Short version: It says every discontinuous function has an elementary antiderivative.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The theorem has hypotheses and does not guarantee elementary formulas.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The theorem has hypotheses and does not guarantee elementary formulas.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Under suitable conditions, differentiating an integral with a variable upper limit recovers the integrand.

feedback.sus: This one is legit. Under suitable conditions, differentiating an integral with a variable upper limit recovers the integrand.

feedback.sus: This one is legit. It connects accumulation by integration with antiderivatives.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: does continuity at a point guarantee differentiability there?

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: what is substitution in integration designed to reverse?

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: in Euclidean space, what does the gradient of a differentiable scalar field indicate?

questions.prompt: No searching: does a function always equal its Taylor series wherever all derivatives exist?
